Lab 4 | PAF 516

PAF 516 | M4 Assignment

Setup & Packages

# ── Package Setup ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# R packages update frequently and can break working code. We have saved
# the exact package versions used to build this lab. Run the block below
# ONCE the first time you open this file, then comment it out.
# if (!requireNamespace("renv", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("renv")
# download.file(
#   url      = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/AntJam-Howell/paf-516-labs/main/Lab4/renv.lock",
#   destfile = "renv.lock", mode = "wb"
# )
# renv::restore(lockfile = "renv.lock", prompt = FALSE)
# After running once, comment out the four lines above.
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

library(tidyverse)    # Data wrangling and ggplot2
library(tidycensus)   # Pull ACS data with geometry
library(sf)           # Simple features for spatial data
library(spdep)        # Spatial dependence: weights, Moran's I, LISA
library(viridis)      # Perceptually uniform color scales
library(patchwork)    # Combine multiple ggplots side-by-side
New package: spdep

The spdep package (spatial dependence) provides the tools for this entire lab: building neighbor relationships, computing spatial weights, running Moran’s I tests, and classifying local clusters. Install it with install.packages("spdep") if you have not already.

Step 1: Pull Maricopa County CBG Data & Build Hardship Index

By now you have done this four times. We pull the same five ACS hardship variables used in Lab 1, but this time at the Census Block Group (CBG) level for Maricopa County. CBGs are smaller than tracts (roughly 600–3,000 people), giving us finer spatial resolution to detect local clustering.

# Define the ACS variables for our economic hardship index.
# Each pair is a numerator/denominator for computing a rate,
# except median_income which is a single estimate.
hardship_vars <- c(
  pov_below50  = "C17002_002",   # Income-to-poverty ratio under .50
  pov_50to99   = "C17002_003",   # Income-to-poverty ratio .50 to .99
  pov_den      = "C17002_001",   # Total population (poverty universe)
  unemp_num    = "B23025_005",   # Unemployed population
  unemp_den    = "B23025_002",   # Population in labor force
  no_hs_num    = "B15003_002",   # No high school diploma (less than 9th grade)
  no_hs_den    = "B15003_001",   # Total population 25+
  no_ins_num   = "B27010_017",   # Without health insurance
  no_ins_den   = "B27010_001",   # Total pop (insurance universe)
  median_income = "B19013_001"   # Median household income
)

# Pull at block group level for Maricopa County, AZ
cbg_raw <- get_acs(
  geography = "block group",
  variables = hardship_vars,
  state     = "AZ",
  county    = "Maricopa",
  year      = 2023,
  survey    = "acs5",
  geometry  = TRUE,
  progress_bar = FALSE
)

# Fallback — uncomment if API unavailable:
# cbg_raw <- readRDS("../data/cbg_hardship_maricopa_2023.rds")
# OR via URL:
# cbg_raw <- readRDS(url("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/AntJam-Howell/paf-516-labs/main/Lab4/data/cbg_hardship_maricopa_2023.rds"))
#
# NOTE: The fallback .rds uses slightly different variable names.
# After loading, recode them to match the named vector above:
# cbg_raw <- cbg_raw %>%
#   mutate(variable = recode(variable,
#     pct_unemp_num = "unemp_num", pct_unemp_den = "unemp_den",
#     pct_no_hs_num = "no_hs_num", pct_no_hs_den = "no_hs_den",
#     pct_no_ins_num = "no_ins_num", pct_no_ins_den = "no_ins_den"))
# Reshape from long to wide: one row per CBG, one column per variable
cbg_wide <- cbg_raw %>%
  select(-moe) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = variable, values_from = estimate)

# Compute rates from numerator/denominator pairs
cbg_wide <- cbg_wide %>%
  mutate(
    poverty_rate = ( pov_below50 + pov_50to99 ) / pov_den,
    unemp_rate   = unemp_num / unemp_den,
    no_hs_rate   = no_hs_num / no_hs_den,
    no_ins_rate  = no_ins_num / no_ins_den
  )

# Standardize all five indicators to z-scores, then reverse median income
# (high income = LOW hardship, so we flip the sign)
cbg_index <- cbg_wide %>%
  filter(!is.na(poverty_rate),
         !is.na(unemp_rate),
         !is.na(no_hs_rate),
         !is.na(no_ins_rate),
         !is.na(median_income)) %>%
  mutate(
    z_poverty  = as.numeric(scale(poverty_rate)),
    z_unemp   = as.numeric(scale(unemp_rate)),
    z_no_hs   = as.numeric(scale(no_hs_rate)),
    z_no_ins  = as.numeric(scale(no_ins_rate)),
    z_income  = -1 * as.numeric(scale(median_income))
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    hardship_index = (z_poverty + z_unemp + z_no_hs + z_no_ins + z_income) / 5
  )
# Quick choropleth to see the spatial pattern before we test it statistically
ggplot(cbg_index) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = hardship_index), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(option = "inferno", name = "Hardship\nIndex") +
  labs(
    title    = "Economic Hardship Index — Maricopa County CBGs",
    subtitle = "Higher values = greater hardship (z-score composite)",
    caption  = "Source: ACS 2019-2023 5-Year Estimates"
  ) +
  theme_void()

The map shows a clear visual pattern: high-hardship CBGs appear to cluster together in certain parts of the county, particularly in south Phoenix and parts of the west valley. But is this clustering statistically significant, or could it be random? That is the question spatial autocorrelation answers.

Step 2: Build Spatial Weights

Before we can test for spatial clustering, we need to define what “neighbor” means. We use queen contiguity: two CBGs are neighbors if they share any boundary point (including corners), similar to how a queen moves in chess. The alternative is rook contiguity, which only counts shared edges (not corners). Queen is the standard default because it captures more neighbor relationships.

# CRITICAL: st_make_valid() fixes any geometry errors (self-intersections,
# invalid rings) in the shapefile. Without this, poly2nb() will often fail
# or produce incorrect neighbor lists.
cbg_index <- st_make_valid(cbg_index)

# poly2nb() — "polygons to neighbors"
# Identifies which CBGs share a boundary. Returns a "nb" (neighbor) object:
# a list where element i contains the indices of all neighbors of polygon i.
# queen=TRUE means corners count as shared boundaries (the default).
nb <- poly2nb(cbg_index, queen = TRUE)

# nb2listw() — "neighbors to list of weights"
# Converts the neighbor list into a spatial weights object that Moran's I needs.
# style="W" means ROW-STANDARDIZED weights: each neighbor gets weight 1/n
# where n is the number of neighbors for that polygon. This means each CBG's
# neighbors collectively contribute a weight of 1.0, regardless of how many
# neighbors it has.
# zero.policy=TRUE: some CBGs may be "islands" with no contiguous neighbors
# (e.g., disconnected polygons). Without this flag, the function would error.
# With it, islands are allowed but excluded from calculations.
lw <- nb2listw(nb, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)
Row-standardization explained

If CBG A has 3 neighbors, each gets weight 1/3. If CBG B has 6 neighbors, each gets weight 1/6. This way, the spatial lag (weighted average of neighbors) is a true average regardless of how many neighbors a polygon has. Without row-standardization, CBGs with more neighbors would have disproportionate influence.

Step 3: Global Moran’s I

Global Moran’s I is a single summary statistic for the entire study area. It answers: “Overall, is the hardship index spatially clustered, randomly distributed, or dispersed?”

  • I near +1 = strong positive spatial autocorrelation (clustering: similar values near each other)
  • I near 0 = spatial randomness (no pattern)
  • I near -1 = spatial dispersion (checkerboard: dissimilar values near each other)
# moran.test() computes Global Moran's I and tests significance.
# The null hypothesis is spatial randomness (CSR = complete spatial randomness).
# zero.policy=TRUE handles any island CBGs.
moran_global <- moran.test(
  cbg_index$hardship_index,
  lw,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

moran_global

    Moran I test under randomisation

data:  cbg_index$hardship_index  
weights: lw    

Moran I statistic standard deviate = 42.844, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: greater
sample estimates:
Moran I statistic       Expectation          Variance 
     0.4741872080     -0.0003763643      0.0001226922 
# Extract key values for interpretation
cat("Moran's I statistic:", round(moran_global$estimate["Moran I statistic"], 4), "\n")
Moran's I statistic: 0.4742 
cat("Expected I under randomness:", round(moran_global$estimate["Expectation"], 4), "\n")
Expected I under randomness: -4e-04 
cat("p-value:", format.pval(moran_global$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")
p-value: <2e-16

Interpretation: The Global Moran’s I is positive and the p-value is extremely small (well below 0.05), meaning we reject the null hypothesis of spatial randomness. Hardship values are significantly clustered in Maricopa County — CBGs with high hardship tend to be surrounded by other high-hardship CBGs, and vice versa.

Step 4: Moran Scatter Plot

The Moran scatter plot visualizes the relationship between each CBG’s hardship value (x-axis) and the average hardship of its neighbors — the spatial lag (y-axis). The slope of the fitted line equals Global Moran’s I.

The four quadrants define the type of spatial association for each observation:

  • HH (upper-right): High value surrounded by high neighbors (hot spot)
  • LL (lower-left): Low value surrounded by low neighbors (cold spot)
  • HL (lower-right): High value surrounded by low neighbors (spatial outlier)
  • LH (upper-left): Low value surrounded by high neighbors (spatial outlier)
# moran.plot() creates the scatter plot with quadrant lines at the means.
# Points in the upper-right (HH) and lower-left (LL) quadrants drive
# positive spatial autocorrelation. Points in HL and LH work against it.
moran.plot(
  cbg_index$hardship_index,
  lw,
  zero.policy = TRUE,
  xlab = "Hardship Index (standardized)",
  ylab = "Spatial Lag of Hardship Index",
  main = "Moran Scatter Plot — Economic Hardship",
  pch  = 20,
  col  = "steelblue"
)

Most points fall in the HH and LL quadrants, confirming positive spatial autocorrelation. The relatively few points in the HL and LH quadrants are spatial outliers — CBGs that differ markedly from their neighbors.

Step 5: Local Moran’s I (LISA)

Global Moran’s I tells us clustering exists overall, but not where. Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA) decompose the global statistic into a value for each CBG, telling us:

  1. Whether that specific CBG is part of a significant cluster
  2. What type of cluster it belongs to (HH, LL, HL, or LH)
# localmoran() returns a matrix with one row per CBG and columns:
#   Ii      = local Moran's I for that CBG
#   E.Ii    = expected value under randomness
#   Var.Ii  = variance
#   Z.Ii    = z-score
#   Pr(z != E(Ii)) = p-value (two-sided test)
lisa <- localmoran(
  cbg_index$hardship_index,
  lw,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

# Attach LISA results to the spatial data frame
cbg_index$lisa_i     <- lisa[, "Ii"]
cbg_index$lisa_z     <- lisa[, "Z.Ii"]
cbg_index$lisa_p     <- lisa[, "Pr(z != E(Ii))"]

# Compute the spatial lag (average of neighbors' values) for quadrant classification
cbg_index$lag_hardship <- lag.listw(lw, cbg_index$hardship_index, zero.policy = TRUE)
# Classify each CBG into a LISA category based on two criteria:
# 1. Statistical significance: p-value <= 0.05
# 2. Quadrant: determined by the signs of the value and its spatial lag
#
# The logic:
#   - Center the variable and its lag at their means
#   - If both are above mean: HH (hot spot)
#   - If both are below mean: LL (cold spot)
#   - If value above but lag below: HL (high outlier surrounded by low)
#   - If value below but lag above: LH (low outlier surrounded by high)
#   - If p > 0.05: Not Significant regardless of quadrant

cbg_index <- cbg_index %>%
  mutate(
    # Center at the mean
    val_centered = hardship_index - mean(hardship_index, na.rm = TRUE),
    lag_centered = lag_hardship   - mean(lag_hardship, na.rm = TRUE),

    # Assign quadrant labels
    quadrant = case_when(
      val_centered > 0 & lag_centered > 0 ~ "HH",
      val_centered < 0 & lag_centered < 0 ~ "LL",
      val_centered > 0 & lag_centered < 0 ~ "HL",
      val_centered < 0 & lag_centered > 0 ~ "LH",
      TRUE ~ "NS"
    ),

    # Apply significance filter: only keep label if p <= 0.05
    lisa_cluster = if_else(lisa_p <= 0.05, quadrant, "NS")
  )

# How many CBGs fall into each category?
table(cbg_index$lisa_cluster)

  HH   HL   LH   LL   NS 
 373   24   48  310 1903

Step 6: LISA Cluster Map

This is the payoff map. Each CBG is colored by its LISA classification:

  • Red (HH): Hot spots — high-hardship CBGs surrounded by high-hardship neighbors
  • Blue (LL): Cold spots — low-hardship CBGs surrounded by low-hardship neighbors
  • Light coral (HL): High-hardship outliers in low-hardship areas
  • Light blue (LH): Low-hardship outliers in high-hardship areas
  • Grey (NS): Not statistically significant at p <= 0.05
# Define the color scheme for LISA categories
lisa_colors <- c(
  "HH" = "red",
  "LL" = "blue",
  "HL" = "#F08080",    # light coral
  "LH" = "#ADD8E6",    # light blue
  "NS" = "grey90"
)

# Convert to factor with explicit levels so the legend is ordered correctly
cbg_index$lisa_cluster <- factor(
  cbg_index$lisa_cluster,
  levels = c("HH", "LL", "HL", "LH", "NS")
)

ggplot(cbg_index) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = lisa_cluster), color = "white", linewidth = 0.05) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = lisa_colors,
    labels = c("HH (Hot Spot)",
               "LL (Cold Spot)",
               "HL (High-Low Outlier)",
               "LH (Low-High Outlier)",
               "NS (Not Significant)"),
    name   = "LISA Cluster"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "LISA Cluster Map — Economic Hardship Index",
    subtitle = "Maricopa County CBGs | Queen contiguity, p <= 0.05",
    caption  = "Source: ACS 2019-2023 5-Year Estimates"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
# Side-by-side comparison: raw hardship index vs. LISA clusters
p1 <- ggplot(cbg_index) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = hardship_index), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(option = "inferno", name = "Hardship\nIndex") +
  labs(title = "Hardship Index (Raw Values)") +
  theme_void()

p2 <- ggplot(cbg_index) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = lisa_cluster), color = "white", linewidth = 0.05) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = lisa_colors, name = "LISA\nCluster") +
  labs(title = "LISA Clusters (Significant Only)") +
  theme_void()

# patchwork: the + operator places plots side by side
p1 + p2

The side-by-side comparison shows the power of LISA analysis: the left map shows the raw pattern (which we could see visually), while the right map tells us which parts of that pattern are statistically significant. Many areas that look like they might be clusters turn out to be not significant at the 0.05 level.

Q1 — Interpret the Global Moran’s I and LISA Results

This question requires you to modify the code from the steps above. Recommendation: re-download the Lab 4 Assignment .qmd and rename it with your last name (e.g. Lab4_Assignment_Howell.qmd) before editing. This preserves the master copy as a reference.

Instructions: What is the Global Moran’s I value and is it statistically significant? What does this tell you about the spatial distribution of hardship in Maricopa County? Identify 2–3 specific hot spot areas from the LISA map.

Common Errors & Troubleshooting

Frequently encountered issues

1. poly2nb() fails with topology errors Always run st_make_valid() before building neighbor lists. Invalid geometries (self-intersections, ring errors) are common in Census shapefiles and will cause poly2nb() to crash or produce wrong results.

2. Error: empty neighbour sets found Some CBGs may be “islands” (no shared boundary with any other CBG). Use zero.policy=TRUE in nb2listw(), moran.test(), and localmoran() to handle these. Islands are excluded from spatial calculations rather than causing errors.

3. All LISA clusters show as NS (Not Significant) Check that you are using the correct variable. If the variable has very little spatial variation (e.g., nearly all CBGs have the same value), Moran’s I will be near zero and no local clusters will be significant.

4. Colors do not match the legend Make sure the lisa_cluster column is a factor with levels in the same order as your color vector: c("HH", "LL", "HL", "LH", "NS").

5. lag.listw() returns NA values This happens for island CBGs when zero.policy=TRUE. These CBGs have no neighbors, so no spatial lag can be computed. They will be classified as NS in the LISA analysis.
